title: Make a Sandwich
summary: Assemble a balanced sandwich with proper layering and safe handling.
learnerPromise: You will be able to make a balanced, well-layered sandwich that stays together and tastes good.
whyItMatters: Sandwiches are a quick, portable meal you can customize to your taste and nutrition needs. Good layering keeps the bread from getting soggy and the filling from falling out.
realLifeUses: Packing a lunch for school or work
Making a quick dinner when you are short on time
Creating a snack before sports practice or a hike
Offering a simple meal to a friend or guest
result: A complete sandwich that is balanced, layered well, and ready to eat.
youWillLearn: Choose ingredients that taste good together
Layer ingredients so the sandwich holds together
Use spreads to add flavor and keep bread from drying out
Cut and store a sandwich neatly for later if needed
miniChallenge: Make a sandwich with at least three layers (spread, protein, vegetable). Take a bite and notice if it holds together.
steps: Lay out two slices of bread on a clean surface.
Spread a thin layer on each slice. This adds flavor and creates a barrier against moisture.
Add your protein on one slice. Fold or slice it so it covers the bread evenly.
Add vegetables on top. Pat them dry if they are wet so the bread does not get soggy.
Press the top slice down gently. Cut in half if you want, and enjoy or wrap for later.
completionCriteria: Your sandwich has at least three different ingredients
The layers are even and the sandwich holds together when picked up
The bread is not soggy and the sandwich is safe to eat
commonProblems: Overfilling the sandwich so it falls apart when eating
Putting wet ingredients directly on dry bread, causing sogginess
Not spreading condiments evenly, leading to dry bites
tips: Dry lettuce and tomato with a paper towel before adding them.
Put spreads on both slices of bread for better flavor coverage.
If packing for later, wrap tightly in foil or a reusable wrap to keep it fresh.
